units.name: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government
Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights
Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what natural rights are — life, liberty, and property according to John Locke
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what social contract theory is — a voluntary agreement between people and government
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what popular sovereignty is — government getting its power from the people's consent
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that Locke and Montesquieu were key Enlightenment thinkers who influenced American democracy
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how the Declaration of Independence reflects natural rights and social contract theory
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Declaration of Independence asserts the right to alter or abolish unjust government
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what a republican form of government means — government run by elected representatives
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how the Constitution establishes a representative republic rather than a direct democracy
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what participatory democracy means — where everyday citizens are actively involved in politics
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between participatory, pluralist, and elite democracy models
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what pluralist democracy means — where competing interest groups shape government policy
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what elite democracy means — government controlled by a small group of wealthy, powerful people
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how each model of democracy is reflected in different American political institutions
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what limited government is — constitutional restrictions on government power to protect individual rights
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the tension between government power and individual liberty as a core tension in American democracy
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know weaknesses of the Articles of Confederation, including no national taxing power
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand why the Articles of Confederation were too weak to run the country
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Articles had no national executive or national judiciary
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Articles required unanimous consent for amendments and supermajority for legislation
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Articles gave Congress no power to regulate interstate commerce
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how Shays Rebellion showed the country needed a stronger national government
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Constitutional Convention of 1787 was called because of the failures of the Articles
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Virginia Plan was favoring large states with representation based on population
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the New Jersey Plan was favoring small states with equal representation
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Great Compromise created a two-chamber Congress — the House (based on population) and the Senate (equal representation per state)
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the Three-Fifths Compromise counting enslaved people was 3/5 of a person for representation purposes
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Electoral College as a compromise for selecting the president balancing popular and state influence
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what Federalists were — people who supported the Constitution and wanted a strong national government
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what Anti-Federalists were — people who opposed the Constitution, wanted more state power, and demanded a Bill of Rights
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between Federalist and Anti-Federalist arguments about whether to adopt the Constitution
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that Anti-Federalist demands led to the addition of the Bill of Rights
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Federalist No. 10 argument that political factions (groups with competing interests) will always exist in a free society
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Madisons argument in Federalist No. 10 that a big country with many competing groups actually helps prevent any one group from taking over
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how Federalist No. 10 argues that a representative government channels the public's views through elected representatives
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how Federalist No. 10 supports pluralist democracy by letting many different groups compete for influence
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Brutus No. 1 argument that a large republic would threaten freedom and people's ability to govern themselves
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Brutus No. 1 concern that the Necessary and Proper Clause (the 'elastic clause') could let the federal government expand its power with no real limit
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Brutus No. 1 argument that representatives in a large republic can't really know or represent the people they serve
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between arguments in Federalist No. 10 and Brutus No. 1 about the size of the republic
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Federalist No. 51 argument for separation of powers among three independent branches
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand Madisons argument in Federalist No. 51 that you have to use people's ambition against each other to prevent power grabs
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how checks and balances keep any one branch from getting too powerful according to Federalist No. 51
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the double security concept in Federalist No. 51 — splitting power between federal and state governments gives people double protection
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the three branches of government is legislative, executive, and judicial
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand separation of powers — giving each branch its own specific job
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know specific checks and balances, including veto, override, judicial review, and Senate confirmation
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how checks and balances maintain the balance of power among the three branches
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Article V amendment process — you need 2/3 of Congress to propose and 3/4 of states to approve
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the formal amendment process — Congress (or a convention) proposes, then states ratify
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the difficulty of amending the Constitution shows the Founders wanted the Constitution to be hard to change
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what listed (enumerated) powers are — powers specifically granted to Congress in Article I Section 8
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know examples of listed (enumerated) powers, including taxing, coining money, declaring war, and regulating commerce
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what implied powers are — powers not specifically listed but implied by the Necessary and Proper Clause (also called the 'elastic clause')
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how the Necessary and Proper Clause (the 'elastic clause') enables Congress to make laws to carry out its listed (enumerated) powers
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what reserved powers are — those retained by the states under the Tenth Amendment
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know examples of reserved powers, including education policy, marriage laws, and intrastate commerce
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what shared (concurrent) powers are — powers shared by both federal and state governments
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know examples of shared (concurrent) powers, including taxing, borrowing money, and establishing courts
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Supremacy Clause establishing federal law as supreme over conflicting state law
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Commerce Clause granting Congress power to regulate interstate commerce
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how federalism divides and shares power between national and state governments
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what dual federalism means — a clear division between federal and state powers
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what cooperative federalism means — overlapping federal and state responsibilities
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between dual federalism and cooperative federalism as different models of the federal relationship
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Fourteenth Amendment as extending due process (fair legal procedures) and equal protection to state government actions
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand the Full Faith and Credit Clause requiring states to honor other states public acts and records
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand McCulloch v. Maryland ruling that Congress has implied powers (powers not written out but suggested by the Constitution) under the Necessary and Proper Clause (the 'elastic clause')
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand McCulloch v. Maryland ruling that states cannot tax federal institutions under the Supremacy Clause
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how McCulloch v. Maryland expanded federal power and supported a broad interpretation of the Constitution
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand United States v. Lopez ruling that the Gun-Free School Zones Act exceeded Congress Commerce Clause power
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how United States v. Lopez limited federal power by narrowing the scope of the Commerce Clause
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between McCulloch v. Maryland and United States v. Lopez as expanding vs limiting federal power
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what categorical grants (federal money with strict rules on how to spend it) means — federal funds given to states for specific narrowly defined purposes
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what block grants (federal money states can spend more flexibly) means — federal funds given to states for broad policy areas with state discretion
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what mandates are — federal requirements imposed on state and local governments
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I can explain the differences between categorical grants (federal money with strict rules on how to spend it) and block grants (federal money states can spend more flexibly) in terms of federal control vs state flexibility
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what unfunded mandates (federal requirements states must follow but don't get money for) is — federal requirements on states that do not include funding
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how grants and mandates shape the balance of power in the federal system
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know what devolution means — the transfer of responsibilities from the federal government to state and local governments
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I understand how revenue sharing provides federal funds to states with few restrictions
skills.unitName: Unit 1: Foundations of American Democracy

skills.description: I know that the House is having 435 members divided up based on state population with two-year terms
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I can explain the differences between the structure, powers, and characteristics of the House and Senate
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the Senate is having 100 members with two per state serving six-year terms
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the House starts tax/spending bills and the Senate approves treaties and confirms presidential appointments
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the House has the sole power to impeach (formally charge) and the Senate has the sole power to hold the impeachment trial
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the Speaker of the House is the presiding officer and most powerful member of the House
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the Vice President is President of the Senate who casts tie-breaking votes
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that Senate Majority Leader is the most powerful member in setting the Senate agenda
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that Senators represent entire states while House members represent specific congressional districts
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the legislative process from bill introduction through committee, floor debate, and presidential action
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the role of committees in shaping legislation through hearings, markups, and reporting bills
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know types of congressional committees, including standing, select, joint, and conference committees
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the role of conference committees in reconciling House and Senate versions of bills
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the House Rules Committee as controlling the terms of debate and amendments for bills on the House floor
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that Senate debate is generally unlimited unlike the more structured House
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what a filibuster means — a Senate tactic of extended debate used to delay or prevent a vote on legislation
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that cloture requires 60 Senate votes to end a filibuster
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the filibuster gives the Senate minority party power to block legislation
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that both chambers must pass identical versions of a bill before it is sent to the president
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents options upon receiving a bill: sign, veto, pocket veto, or let it become law
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Congress power of the purse including taxing, spending, and approving the federal budget
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the Senates role in confirming presidential appointments including judicial nominees
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what logrolling (trading votes — 'I'll vote for yours if you vote for mine') means — the trading of votes among legislators to pass bills of mutual interest
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what pork barrel legislation means — spending directed at a specific district to benefit constituents
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how partisan politics and increasing polarization affect congressional behavior and gridlock
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the delegate model where representatives vote according to constituent preferences
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the trustee model where representatives use their own judgment when voting
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the politico model as a combination of delegate and trustee approaches depending on the issue
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I can explain the differences between delegate, trustee, and politico models of representation
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how divided government can lead to legislative gridlock
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how party leadership including whips and caucuses influences congressional voting behavior
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Baker v. Carr ruling that legislative redistricting is a something the courts can decide (under the Equal Protection Clause)
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the one person one vote principle requiring roughly equal population in legislative districts
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know the formal constitutional powers of the president in Article II
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents role as commander in chief of the armed forces
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents veto power and the requirement of a two-thirds vote in both chambers to override
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents power to negotiate treaties requiring two-thirds Senate approval
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents appointment power for federal judges, cabinet members, and ambassadors
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents power to grant pardons and reprieves for federal offenses
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents power to receive ambassadors and conduct foreign policy
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the presidents duty to give Congress information on the State of the Union
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know informal presidential powers, including executive orders, executive agreements, and signing statements
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how executive orders direct federal agencies and have the force of law without congressional approval
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what executive agreements is — international agreements made without Senate treaty ratification
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what signing statements is — presidential interpretations of legislation that may influence implementation
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Federalist No. 70 argument that a single executive provides energy in the executive branch
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Hamiltons argument that a unitary executive ensures decisiveness, secrecy, and dispatch in governance
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Hamiltons argument that a single executive allows clear accountability to the public
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress checks the president through the power to override vetoes
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress checks the president through the power of the purse
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Senate checks the president through the treaty ratification power
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Senate checks the president through the confirmation of appointments
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress checks the president through the impeachment and removal process
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the War Powers Resolution of 1973 requires presidential notification and limits troop deployment
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the ongoing tension between presidential war powers and congressional authority to declare war
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how presidential power has expanded beyond original constitutional provisions over time
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how executive orders have expanded presidential domestic policy influence
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how presidential power expands during national crises and emergencies
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how executive agreements have increased presidential power in foreign affairs
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the growth of the Executive Office of the President as reflecting expanding presidential responsibilities
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the bully pulpit as the presidents ability to use media access to shape public opinion and policy
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the State of the Union address allows the president to set the legislative agenda
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how presidential communication strategies including press conferences and social media influence policy
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how presidents use the mandate of the people to pressure Congress to pass their agenda
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know the structure of federal courts, including district courts, courts of appeals, and the Supreme Court
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the difference between original jurisdiction and appellate jurisdiction
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the Supreme Court has both limited original jurisdiction and broad appellate jurisdiction
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that federal judges serve lifetime appointments during good behavior under Article III
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how lifetime tenure promotes judicial independence from political pressure
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what Marbury v. Madison is — establishing the principle of judicial review
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what judicial review is — the power of courts to strike down laws or executive actions as unconstitutional
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how judicial review serves as a check on the legislative and executive branches
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Federalist No. 78 argument that the judiciary is the least dangerous branch lacking force and will
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand Hamiltons argument in Federalist No. 78 for judicial independence through lifetime tenure
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what Hamiltons argument in Federalist No. 78 for judicial review is — essential to a limited constitution
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I can explain the differences between Federalist No. 78 arguments about judicial power with debates over judicial activism
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what judicial activism means — courts broadly interpreting the Constitution to shape social policy
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what judicial restraint means — courts deferring to the elected branches and adhering to precedent
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I can explain the differences between judicial activism (judges making bold decisions) and judicial restraint (judges deferring to elected branches) as competing judicial philosophies
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand stare decisis — the principle that courts follow established precedent in deciding cases
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Supreme Court can overturn its own precedent in extraordinary circumstances
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the process of selecting Supreme Court cases through writs of certiorari
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the rule of four as requiring four justices to agree to hear a case
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the role of oral arguments in Supreme Court proceedings
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the role of amicus curiae briefs (friend-of-the-court arguments from outside groups) as friend of the court briefs filed by interested parties
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the difference between majority opinions, concurring opinions, and dissenting opinions
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that majority opinions establish binding precedent as the law of the land
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Solicitor General (the government's top lawyer at the Supreme Court) represents the federal government before the Supreme Court
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress can check the judiciary through proposing constitutional amendments
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress can check the judiciary by changing the size or jurisdiction of federal courts
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Senate confirmation process serves as a check on judicial appointments
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the president checks the judiciary through the power to appoint federal judges
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that implementation of court decisions depends on cooperation from the executive and legislative branches
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the debate about whether judges should interpret the Constitution based on original meaning or as a living document
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the federal bureaucracy is including cabinet departments, independent agencies, and regulatory commissions
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the structure of the federal bureaucracy including the fifteen cabinet departments
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the Executive Office of the President as containing key advisory bodies like the NSC and OMB
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what independent regulatory commissions is — agencies with quasi-legislative and quasi-judicial powers
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the bureaucracy implements federal policy through rulemaking and enforcement
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand the merit system of civil service based on qualifications rather than political patronage
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Pendleton Civil Service Act replaced the spoils system with merit-based hiring
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know what bureaucratic discretion is — the power of agencies to interpret and apply legislation
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how bureaucratic agencies issue regulations through the rulemaking process that have the force of law
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how iron triangles (tight relationships between agencies, interest groups, and congressional committees) connect bureaucratic agencies, congressional committees, and interest groups
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how issue networks (looser, broader groups of people who all care about the same policy area) are broader and more fluid policy communities than iron triangles (tight relationships between agencies, interest groups, and congressional committees)
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I can explain the differences between iron triangles (tight relationships between agencies, interest groups, and congressional committees) and issue networks (looser, broader groups of people who all care about the same policy area) as models of policymaking relationships
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand congressional oversight of the bureaucracy through hearings, investigations, and appropriations
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how Congress uses the power of the purse to control bureaucratic agency funding
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the president holds the bureaucracy accountable through appointments and executive orders
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the judiciary checks the bureaucracy through judicial review of agency actions and regulations
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how whistleblower protections encourage reporting of bureaucratic waste and abuse
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I understand how the Freedom of Information Act promotes government transparency and accountability
skills.unitName: Unit 2: Interactions Among Branches of Government

skills.description: I know that the Bill of Rights is the first ten amendments to the Constitution
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Bill of Rights originally restricted only the federal government not state governments
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Fourteenth Amendment Due Process Clause is the basis for applying the Bill of Rights to states
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what selective incorporation (applying the Bill of Rights to the states one right at a time) means — the case-by-case process of applying Bill of Rights protections to the states
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that not all Bill of Rights provisions have been incorporated against the states
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Establishment Clause prohibits government from establishing an official religion
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Engel v. Vitale ruling that school-sponsored prayer in public schools violates the Establishment Clause
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that Engel v. Vitale held that government-directed prayer violates the separation of church and state
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Free Exercise Clause is protecting the right to practice ones religion freely
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Wisconsin v. Yoder ruling that Amish families may exempt children from compulsory school after eighth grade
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that Wisconsin v. Yoder held that free exercise of religion outweighed the states interest in education
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the tension between the Establishment Clause and the Free Exercise Clause
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the First Amendment is protecting freedom of speech
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Schenck v. United States ruling that speech creating a clear and present danger is not protected
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the clear and present danger test as a standard for when government may limit speech
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Tinker v. Des Moines ruling that students retain First Amendment rights in public schools
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that student speech can be limited only if it causes substantial disruption to the school environment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know categories of unprotected speech, including incitement, true threats, fighting words, and obscenity
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the ongoing balancing test between protecting free speech and maintaining public order and safety
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the distinction between content-based and content-neutral restrictions on speech
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that symbolic speech such as flag burning and armbands is generally protected by the First Amendment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the First Amendment is protecting freedom of the press
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand New York Times Co. v. United States ruling against prior restraint (government censorship before something is published) of the Pentagon Papers publication
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what prior restraint (government censorship before something is published) means — government censorship prohibiting speech or publication before it occurs
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that prior restraint (government censorship before something is published) carries a heavy presumption of unconstitutionality under the First Amendment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Second Amendment is protecting the right to keep and bear arms
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand McDonald v. City of Chicago ruling that the Second Amendment applies to state and local governments
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the ongoing debate between individual gun rights and government authority to regulate firearms
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that McDonald v. City of Chicago is an example of selective incorporation (applying the Bill of Rights to the states one right at a time) of the Second Amendment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know Fourth Amendment protections against unreasonable searches and seizures
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the exclusionary rule — illegally obtained evidence can't be used in court
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the warrant requirement and probable cause (reasonable belief that a crime occurred) standard under the Fourth Amendment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know Fifth Amendment protections, including due process (fair legal procedures), protection from double jeopardy, and self-incrimination
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how the Fifth Amendment due process (fair legal procedures) clause protects individuals from arbitrary federal government action
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know the Sixth Amendment rights, including speedy trial, impartial jury, and right to counsel
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Gideon v. Wainwright ruling that states must provide attorneys to defendants who cannot afford them
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that Gideon v. Wainwright incorporated the Sixth Amendment right to counsel against the states
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that the Eighth Amendment prohibits excessive bail, excessive fines, and cruel and unusual punishment
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand ongoing debates about the application of cruel and unusual punishment to the death penalty
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how due process (fair legal procedures) protections balance the need for government power with individual rights
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the right to privacy as derived from multiple amendments including the First, Third, Fourth, Fifth, and Ninth
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what Roe v. Wade is — establishing a constitutional right to privacy regarding abortion
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the ongoing debate over the scope and limits of the right to privacy
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the Fourteenth Amendment Equal Protection Clause as requiring states to provide equal treatment under the law
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how the Equal Protection Clause has been used to combat racial, gender, and other forms of discrimination
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Brown v. Board of Education ruling that racial segregation in public schools violates equal protection
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that Brown overturned the separate but equal doctrine established in Plessy v. Ferguson
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the Civil Rights Movement as a social movement demanding legal equality for African Americans
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand Letter from Birmingham Jail arguments that individuals have a moral responsibility to disobey unjust laws
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand MLKs argument distinguishing between just laws and unjust laws based on moral and natural law
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand MLKs critique of the white moderate who preferred order over justice
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what MLKs argument for nonviolent direct action is — a means of creating constructive tension for change
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the Civil Rights Act of 1964 as prohibiting discrimination based on race, color, religion, sex, or national origin
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the Voting Rights Act of 1965 as prohibiting racial discrimination in voting and eliminating literacy tests
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what Title IX of the Education Amendments of 1972 is — prohibiting sex-based discrimination in federally funded education
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how government legislation has responded to social movements to expand civil rights protections
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how the womens rights movement used the Equal Protection Clause to challenge gender discrimination
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the tension between majority rule and protection of minority rights in a democratic system
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand how the judiciary serves as a protector of minority rights against potential tyranny of the majority
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what affirmative action means — policies that consider race or gender to benefit underrepresented groups
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I understand the ongoing debate over whether affirmative action promotes equality or constitutes reverse discrimination
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know that Engel v. Vitale is an example of selective incorporation (applying the Bill of Rights to the states one right at a time) of the Establishment Clause
skills.unitName: Unit 3: Civil Liberties and Civil Rights

skills.description: I know what political ideology is — a consistent set of beliefs about what government should and shouldn't do
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that the liberal-conservative spectrum (left to right) is the primary ideological framework in American politics
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that liberals generally favor government intervention in the economy and protection of individual civil liberties
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that conservatives generally favor limited government in the economy and traditional social values
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can explain the differences between liberal and conservative positions on the role and size of government
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what libertarianism means — favoring minimal government intervention in both economic and social issues
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what populism means — supporting the interests of ordinary people often favoring government economic intervention and traditional values
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can place political ideologies on a spectrum from liberal to moderate to conservative to libertarian
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how core American values including individualism, equality of opportunity, free enterprise, and rule of law shape political attitudes
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how Americans can share core values but disagree on how government should implement them
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what political socialization (how people develop their political views) means — the process through which individuals develop their political beliefs and values
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that family is the primary and most influential agent of political socialization (how people develop their political views)
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that schools and education is agents of political socialization (how people develop their political views) that promote civic values
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that peers, media, religious institutions, and social groups is agents of political socialization (how people develop their political views)
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how demographic factors including race and ethnicity influence political attitudes and party identification
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how gender influences political attitudes with women more likely to support the Democratic Party
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how religious beliefs and religious denomination influence political ideology and party affiliation
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how education level and income influence political attitudes and participation
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how political socialization (how people develop their political views) produces generational differences in political attitudes and behavior
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how political ideologies shift over time in response to events, demographics, and cultural change
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the concept of realignment as a dramatic long-term shift in party coalitions and dominant ideology
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how globalization and technological change have influenced American political ideologies
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how major events like the Great Depression shifted public attitudes toward greater government involvement
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how events like the Vietnam War and Watergate decreased public trust in government
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how the September 11 attacks shifted attitudes about national security and civil liberties
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the long-term trend of declining trust in government institutions since the 1960s
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the relationship between political events and shifts in ideological identification among the public
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what public opinion means — the aggregate of attitudes and beliefs held by the adult population about political issues
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how scientific polling uses random sampling to accurately measure public opinion
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the importance of random sampling to ensure a poll is representative of the broader population
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what sampling error (margin of error) is — the range within which the true population value likely falls
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know factors affecting poll reliability, including sample size, question wording, question order, and timing
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can make sense of a polls margin of error to determine whether differences between groups or candidates are statistically meaningful
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know potential sources of bias in polling, including leading questions, selection bias, and response bias
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can evaluate the credibility and methodological validity of public opinion polls and survey data
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand public opinion data presented in charts, tables, graphs, and infographics
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can draw valid conclusions from polling data about trends in public opinion across demographic groups
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that the Democratic Party platform generally aligns with liberal ideology on economic and social issues
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know that the Republican Party platform generally aligns with conservative ideology on economic and social issues
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can explain the differences between the policy platforms of the Democratic and Republican parties on specific issues
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how party platforms reflect ideological positions and serve as governing blueprints
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how competing ideologies lead to different policy proposals on the same policy issue
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how ideology influences government decisions on taxes, spending, regulation, and social programs
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand liberal support for progressive taxation where higher earners pay higher tax rates
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand liberal support for government regulation of the economy to protect consumers and workers
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand conservative support for lower taxes and reduced government regulation to promote economic growth
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand conservative support for free market solutions and limited government intervention in the economy
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what supply-side economics is — the belief that tax cuts for businesses and individuals stimulate growth
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I know what Keynesian economics is — the belief that government spending can stimulate the economy during downturns
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can explain the differences between supply-side and Keynesian approaches to fiscal and economic policy
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how ideological differences shape debates over the federal budget, national debt, and entitlement programs
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the debate between expanding and reducing the social safety net including Social Security, Medicare, and Medicaid
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand liberal positions on social issues including support for expanding healthcare access and environmental regulation
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand conservative positions on social issues including emphasis on traditional values and personal responsibility
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I can explain the differences between liberal and conservative policy positions on healthcare, immigration, and environmental policy
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand how ideological differences shape major policy debates in contemporary American politics
skills.unitName: Unit 4: American Political Ideologies and Beliefs

skills.description: I understand the historical expansion of suffrage through constitutional amendments
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Fifteenth Amendment as prohibiting denial of voting rights based on race
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Nineteenth Amendment as establishing womens right to vote
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Twenty-Fourth Amendment as abolishing poll taxes in federal elections
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Twenty-Sixth Amendment as lowering the voting age to eighteen
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Voting Rights Act of 1965 as eliminating barriers such as literacy tests for minority voters
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what rational-choice voting (picking whoever benefits you most) means — selecting the candidate whose policies maximize ones self-interest
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I can explain the differences between rational-choice, retrospective, prospective, and party-line voting (voting for one party straight down the ballot) models
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what retrospective voting (voting based on how things have been going) means — basing vote choice on past performance of the incumbent or party
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what prospective voting (voting based on what you think a candidate will do) means — basing vote choice on predictions about a candidates future policy performance
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what party-line voting (voting for one party straight down the ballot) means — voting for candidates of one political party for all offices on the ballot
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know factors that increase voter turnout, including higher education, higher income, and older age
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand demographic patterns in voter turnout across different groups
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what political efficacy (believing your vote and participation actually matter) means — the belief that ones participation in politics can make a difference
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how high political efficacy (believing your vote and participation actually matter) increases voter turnout and political engagement
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know that voter turnout is consistently higher in presidential elections than in midterm elections
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know structural barriers to voting, including registration requirements, voter ID laws, and felony disenfranchisement
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how institutional factors like registration deadlines and polling access affect voter turnout
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how reforms such as the Motor Voter Act, early voting, and mail-in voting aim to increase turnout
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the implications of low voter turnout for democratic representation and legitimacy
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the voter turnout gap between racial and ethnic groups and efforts to address it
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what political parties are — organizations that recruit candidates, mobilize voters, and seek to win elections
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the key functions of parties including nominating candidates, running campaigns, and organizing government
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the two-party system has dominated American politics since the early republic
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how winner-take-all single-member district plurality elections reinforce the two-party system
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know the three components of a political party: party in the electorate, party organization, and party in government
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what party identification means — a voters long-term psychological attachment to a political party
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know that party identification is the strongest single predictor of how a person will vote
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what critical elections and party realignments is — major long-term shifts in party coalitions
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the New Deal realignment as shifting African Americans, urban workers, and the South into different coalitions
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what dealignment is — a weakening of party ties and an increase in the number of independent voters
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how candidate-centered campaigns have weakened traditional party organizational influence
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know barriers to third-party success, including winner-take-all elections, ballot access laws, and debate exclusion
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know that third parties often bring new issues to public attention that major parties then co-opt
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the spoiler effect as third-party candidates drawing votes from a major-party candidate in close elections
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how major parties absorb third-party issues to maintain their dominant position
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what interest groups are — organizations that try to influence public policy without seeking elected office
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand interest group strategies including lobbying, litigation, grassroots mobilization, and electioneering
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how interest groups use direct lobbying to influence members of Congress and executive agencies
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how interest groups use litigation and amicus curiae briefs (friend-of-the-court arguments from outside groups) to influence judicial decisions
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how interest groups mobilize members through grassroots campaigns, protests, and letter-writing
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I can tell the difference between economic groups, public interest groups, single-issue groups, and ideological groups
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the free-rider problem (people benefiting from a group's work without helping) as individuals benefiting from group efforts without contributing
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how PACs raise money from members and contribute to candidates campaigns within legal limits
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how Super PACs can raise and spend unlimited funds on independent expenditures
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand Citizens United v. FEC ruling that independent political spending by corporations and unions is protected speech
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how Citizens United v. FEC increased the role of outside money in American elections
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the revolving door (people going back and forth between government jobs and lobbying) as movement of individuals between government positions and lobbying firms
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the presidential election process from primaries through the general election
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how primaries and caucuses allow party members to select their partys presidential nominee
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I can explain the differences between open primaries allowing any voter to participate with closed primaries limited to registered party members
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Electoral College as the constitutional mechanism for electing the president
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know that a candidate needs 270 of 538 electoral votes to win the presidency
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know that most states use a winner-take-all system for allocating electoral votes
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the Electoral College can produce a president who loses the national popular vote
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the Electoral College system causes candidates to focus on competitive swing states
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the role of national party conventions in formally nominating the presidential and vice-presidential candidates
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the primary process frontloads early states giving them disproportionate influence
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the advantages of incumbency in congressional elections including high reelection rates
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know incumbency advantages, including name recognition, franking privilege (free mailings to constituents), casework, and fundraising advantages
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what gerrymandering (drawing district lines to give one party an advantage) means — the manipulation of legislative district boundaries for political advantage
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand Shaw v. Reno ruling that racially motivated gerrymandering (drawing district lines based on race) must be subject to strict scrutiny (the toughest legal test for government actions)
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I can tell the difference between partisan gerrymandering (drawing district lines to give one party an advantage) and racial gerrymandering (drawing district lines based on race to dilute or concentrate minority voting power)
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how gerrymandering (drawing district lines to give one party an advantage) creates safe seats and reduces electoral competition
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how midterm elections frequently result in the presidents party losing congressional seats
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how modern campaigns are candidate-centered relying on personal organizations rather than party machines
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the role of campaign consultants, pollsters, focus groups, and media strategists in modern campaigns
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how campaigns use microtargeting (using data to send personalized messages to specific voters) and data analytics to identify and mobilize potential supporters
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how social media platforms have transformed campaign communication and voter engagement
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Federal Election Campaign Act as establishing contribution limits and disclosure requirements
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the role of the Federal Election Commission in administering and enforcing federal campaign finance law
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the Bipartisan Campaign Reform Act as banning soft money contributions to national party committees
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how Citizens United v. FEC struck down portions of BCRA restricting corporate independent expenditures
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the ongoing debate between regulating campaign finance to prevent corruption and protecting political speech
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the role of the media as a linkage institution (something that connects the government to the people) connecting government to the public
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how media agenda-setting (deciding which issues get attention) influences which issues the public and government consider important
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what gatekeeping (deciding what gets covered and what doesn't) is — the medias power to choose which stories to cover and which to ignore
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I know what horse-race journalism (covering who's winning instead of actual policy) is — coverage focused on who is winning rather than substantive policy issues
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the watchdog role of the media in investigating and reporting on government actions
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the decline of traditional media sources has affected investigative journalism
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how the rise of cable news, internet media, and social media has fragmented the media landscape
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how media fragmentation has contributed to political polarization and echo chambers
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the impact of ideological media outlets on reinforcing existing political beliefs
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how misinformation and disinformation spread through digital and social media platforms
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand the transition from broadcast network news dominance to a diverse and decentralized media environment
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation

skills.description: I understand how changes in media consumption patterns affect political knowledge and civic engagement
skills.unitName: Unit 5: Political Participation
